html:
VI

‚ÄúThese are the things I wanted you to see,‚Äù said Gert Gram, rising. He had been on his knees, looking for something on the lower shelf of his safe.

Jenny pushed the sketchbooks aside and pulled the electric lamp nearer. He wiped the dust from the big portfolio and placed it before her.

‚ÄúI have not shown these to anybody for a great many years, or looked at them myself, but I have been wanting you to see them for some time‚ÅÝ‚Äîin fact, from the day I called on you at your studio. When you came here to look at Helge‚Äôs picture I meant to ask you if you cared to see them, and all the time you were working close by here I had it in my mind.

‚ÄúIt is strange to think, Jenny, that here in this little office I have buried all my dreams of youth. There in the safe they lie like corpses in their tomb, and I myself go about a dead and forgotten artist.‚Äù

Jenny said nothing. Gram sometimes used expressions that were rather too sentimental, she thought, although she knew that the bitter feelings which dictated them were real enough. In a sudden impulse she bent forward and stroked his grey hair.

Gram bowed his head as if to prolong the slight caress‚ÅÝ‚Äîand without looking at her, untied the portfolio with trembling hands.

She realized in surprise that her own hands shook as she took the first sheet from him, and she felt a strange fear and oppression at heart, as of a danger threatening. She was suddenly afraid when she realized that she did not want anybody to know of her visit and that she dared not tell Helge about it. At the mere thought of her lover she became depressed; she had long since consciously stopped analysing her real feelings for him. She did not want to heed the foreboding that crossed her mind at this moment, not to let herself be disturbed by inquiring into Gert Gram‚Äôs feelings for her.

She turned over the sheets of the portfolio with the dreams of his youth; it was a melancholy business. He had told her about this work often when they were alone, and she understood that he thought he had been born an artist for the sake of this and nothing else. The pictures hanging in his home he called the amateur work of a conscientious and diligent pupil, but these‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey were his own. They were illustrations to Landstad‚Äôs Folksongs.

At first sight these big sheets with frames of Roman foliage and ornate black-letter writing were good enough. The colouring was pure and effective in most of them, in some really fine, but the figures in the vignettes and borders were without style and life, although the miniature drawings were correct in every detail. Some of them were naturalistic, others approaching Italian medieval art to such an extent that Jenny recognized certain Annunciation angels and madonnas in the cloaks of knights and maidens, and the leaves in gold and purple she remembered having seen, in a book of mass in the San Marco library. The words of the songs looked very strange, hand-printed in elegant monastic Latin types. In some of the larger full-page illustrations the composition was baroque, a direct copy of Roman altar pictures. It was an echo of all he had seen and lived among and loved‚ÅÝ‚Äîan echo of the melody of Gert Gram‚Äôs youth; not a single note was his own, but this melody of many notes was resounded in a particularly soft, melancholy tone.

‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt quite like them,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúI can see you don‚Äôt.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, I like them. There is much that is pretty and delicate about them, but, you know‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe searched for the right expression‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúthe effect is a little strange on us, who have seen the same subjects treated differently and so perfectly, that we cannot conceive them treated in another way.‚Äù

He sat opposite her, resting his chin in his hand. By and by he looked up, and she was sad at heart to meet his eyes.

‚ÄúI seem to remember them as being much better than they are,‚Äù he said quietly, trying to smile. ‚ÄúI have not opened this portfolio for many a year, as I told you.‚Äù

‚ÄúI have never quite understood that you were so attracted by the later renaissance and the baroque,‚Äù she said, to divert the conversation.

‚ÄúI am not surprised, Jenny dear, that you don‚Äôt understand it.‚Äù He looked into her face with a melancholy smile. ‚ÄúThere was a time when I believed in myself as an artist, but not so completely that I did not have a slight doubt sometimes, not of succeeding to express what I wanted, but as to what I really wanted to express. I saw that romantic art had had its day and was on the decline‚ÅÝ‚Äîthere was decadence and falsehood all along‚ÅÝ‚Äîand yet in my heart I was devoted to romance, not in painting alone, but in real life. I wanted the Sunday-peasants of romance, although I had lived long enough in the country as a boy to know they did not exist, and when I went abroad it was to the Italy of romance I turned my steps. I know that you and your contemporaries seek beauty in things as they are, tangible and real. To me there was beauty only in the transformation of reality, which had already been done by others. In the eighties there came a new art-creed. I tried to adopt it, but the result was lip-service only, for my heart rebelled against it.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut reality, Gert, is not a fixed conception. It appears different to everyone who sees it. An English painter once said to me: ‚ÄòThere is beauty in everything; only your eyes see it or do not see it.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúI was not made to conceive reality, only the reflection of it in the dreams of others. I lacked entirely the capacity to form a beauty for myself out of the complexity of realities; I knew my own ineffectiveness. When I came to Italy the baroque took my heart and fancy. Can you not understand the agony of my soul on realizing my inefficiency? To have nothing new or personal wherewith to fill up form, only develop the technique in soaring fancies, breakneck foreshortenings, powerful effects of light and shade, and cunningly thought-out compositions. The emptiness of it all is to be hidden under the ecstasy‚ÅÝ‚Äîcontorted faces, twisted limbs, saints, whose only true passion is the dread of their own engulfing doubt, which they try to drown in sickly exaltation. It is the despair of the good, the work of an epigon school wishing to fascinate‚ÅÝ‚Äîmostly themselves.‚Äù

Jenny nodded. ‚ÄúWhat you say, Gert, is at least your own subjective view. I am not so sure that the painters you speak of were not highly pleased with themselves.‚Äù

He laughed and said: ‚ÄúPerhaps they were‚ÅÝ‚Äîand perhaps this is my hobbyhorse because for once I had‚ÅÝ‚Äîas you say‚ÅÝ‚Äîa subjective view.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut the picture of your wife in red is impressionistic, excellent. The more I look at it, the more I like it.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, but that is a solitary instance.‚Äù After a pause ‚ÄúWhen I painted it, she was all the world to me. I was very much in love with her‚ÅÝ‚Äîand I hated her intensely already.‚Äù

‚ÄúWas it because of her you gave up painting?‚Äù asked Jenny.

‚ÄúNo. All our misfortunes are of our own making. I know you have not what they call faith, neither have I‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut I believe in a God, if you like, or a spiritual power which punishes justly.

‚ÄúShe was cashier in a shop in the High Street; I happened to see her there. She was remarkably pretty, as you can see still. One evening when she went home I waited for her and spoke to her. We made friends‚ÅÝ‚Äîand I seduced her,‚Äù he said in a low and harsh voice.

‚ÄúAnd you married her because she was going to have a child?‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI thought so. For twenty-seven years she has tormented you in return. Do you know what I think of the deity you believe in?‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat it is rather relentless.‚Äù

He smiled wearily. ‚ÄúI am not quite so old-fashioned as you may think. I don‚Äôt consider it a sin if two young people, who love and believe in one another, join their lives in a lawful or unlawful way. But in my case I was the abductor. She was innocent when I met her‚ÅÝ‚Äîinnocent in every way. I understood her better than she did herself. I saw that she was passionate, and would be jealous and tyrannical in her love, but I did not care. I was flattered that her passion was for me, that this beautiful girl was all mine, but I never meant to be hers alone, the way I knew she wanted it. I did not exactly mean to leave her, but I thought I should be able to arrange our life in such a way as not to share with her my interests, my work, my real life in fact, as I knew she would want to do. It was stupid of me, knowing that I was weak and she strong and ruthless. I thought that her great passion would give me, who was comparatively cold, a hold on her.

‚ÄúBeyond her great faculty of loving there was nothing in her. She was vain and uneducated, envious and crude. There could be no mental fellowship between us, but I did not miss it; to possess her beauty and her passionate love was all I cared for.‚Äù

He rose and went over to the side where Jenny was sitting. He took both her hands and pressed them against his eyes.

‚ÄúWhat else but misery could I expect from a marriage with her? But we reap as we sow, and I had to marry her. I had a dreadful time of it. At first, when she came to my studio, she was proud to be my mistress, arrogant in her denunciation of old prejudices, declaring that the only life worth living was that of free love. The moment things went wrong she changed her tone. Then it was all about her respectable family in Frederikshald, her unstained virtue, and her good reputation. Many men had wanted her, but she had not listened to anybody, and I was a scoundrel and a wretch if I did not marry her at once. I had nothing to marry on; I had neglected my studies and had learnt nothing but painting. Some months went by‚ÅÝ‚Äîat last I had to apply to my father. My people helped me through. We got married, and two months later Helge arrived.

‚ÄúI had had hopes of a great artistic work‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy folklore illustrations‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut I had to give up my dreams for the reality of making bread and butter. Once I had to come to an agreement with my creditors. She took her share of the struggle and poverty loyally and without complaint‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe would willingly have starved for me and the children. Feeling as I did towards her, it was hard to accept what she gave in working, suffering, and renouncing for my sake.

‚ÄúI had to sacrifice everything I loved; she forced me to give it up inch by inch. From the very first she and my father were mortal enemies. He could not bear his daughter-in-law, and that was a blow to her vanity, so she set about to make trouble between him and me. My father was an official of the old school‚ÅÝ‚Äîa bit narrow and stiff maybe, but right-minded and loyal, noble and good at heart. You would have liked him, I am sure. We had been so much to one another, but our intimacy was put a stop to.

‚ÄúAs for my painting, I understood that I had not the talent I once had imagined, and I lacked energy to make continued efforts when I did not believe in myself‚ÅÝ‚Äîdead tired as I was of the struggle and of my life at her side, which became more and more of a caricature. She reproached me, but secretly she triumphed.

‚ÄúShe was jealous of the children too, if I was fond of them or they were fond of me. She would not share them with me nor me with them.

‚ÄúHer jealousy has grown into a kind of madness as the years have gone by. You have seen it for yourself. She can scarcely bear to see me in the same room with you even when Helge is there.‚Äù

Jenny went to him and laid her hands on his shoulders:

‚ÄúI cannot understand,‚Äù she said‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄúI really cannot‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat you have been able to stand such a life.‚Äù

Gert Gram bent forward, resting his head on her shoulder:

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt understand it myself.‚Äù

When he raised his head and their eyes met, she put her hand to his neck and, overwhelmed by a tender compassion, kissed him on the cheek and forehead.

She felt a sudden fear when she looked down at his face resting on her shoulder, with eyes closed, but the next moment he lifted his head and rose, saying:

‚ÄúThank you, Jenny dear.‚Äù

Gram put the drawings back in their cover and straightened the table.

‚ÄúI hope you will be very, very happy. You are so bright and courageous, so energetic and gifted. Dear child, you are everything I wanted to be, but never was.‚Äù He spoke in a low, absentminded voice.

‚ÄúI think,‚Äù he said a moment later, ‚Äúthat when relations between two people are new, before their life is perfectly accorded, there are many small difficulties to overcome. I wish you could live elsewhere, not in this town. You should be alone, far from your own people‚ÅÝ‚Äîat first at least.‚Äù

‚ÄúHelge has applied for a post in Bergen, as you know,‚Äù said Jenny, and the feeling of despair and anguish again seized her when she thought of him.

‚ÄúDo you never speak to your mother about it? Why don‚Äôt you? Are you not fond of your mother?‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course I am fond of her.‚Äù

‚ÄúI should think it would be a good thing to talk to her about it‚ÅÝ‚Äîget her advice.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is no good asking anybody‚Äôs advice‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI don‚Äôt like to speak to anyone about these things,‚Äù she said, wishing to dismiss the subject.

‚ÄúNo, you are perhaps.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù He had been standing halfway turned to the window. Suddenly his face changed, and he whispered in a state of excitement:

‚ÄúJenny, she is down there in the street!‚Äù

‚ÄúWho?‚Äù

‚ÄúShe‚ÅÝ‚ÄîRebecca!‚Äù

Jenny rose. She felt she could have screamed with exasperation and disgust. She trembled; every fibre of her body was quivering with revolt. She would not be involved in all this‚ÅÝ‚Äîthese wicked, odious suspicions, quarrels, spiteful words, and scenes‚ÅÝ‚Äîno, she would not.

‚ÄúJenny, my child, you are shivering‚ÅÝ‚Äîdon‚Äôt be afraid. I won‚Äôt let her hurt you.‚Äù

‚ÄúAfraid? Far from it.‚Äù She steeled herself at once. ‚ÄúI have been here to fetch you; we have looked at your drawings, and we are now going to your house to supper.‚Äù

‚ÄúShe may not have noticed anything.‚Äù

‚ÄúHeavens! we have nothing to hide. If she had not seen that I am here she will soon get to know it. I am going with you; we must do it for your sake as well as for mine‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo you hear?‚Äù

Gram looked at her: ‚ÄúYes, let us go, then.‚Äù

When they got down in the street Mrs.¬ÝGram was gone.

‚ÄúLet us take the tram, Gert; it is late,‚Äù she said, adding in a sudden temper: ‚ÄúOh, we must stop all this‚ÅÝ‚Äîif only for Helge‚Äôs sake.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGram opened the door. Gert Gram ventured an explanation; Jenny looked frankly into the angry eyes of his wife: ‚ÄúI am sorry Helge is out for the evening. Do you think he will be home early?‚Äù

‚ÄúI am surprised you did not remember it,‚Äù Mrs.¬ÝGram said to her husband. ‚ÄúIt is no pleasure to Miss Winge to sit here with us two old people.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, that is all right,‚Äù said Jenny.

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt remember hearing that Helge was going out this evening,‚Äù said Gram.

‚ÄúFancy your coming without any needlework,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝGram, when they were sitting in the drawing-room after supper. ‚ÄúYou are always so industrious.‚Äù

‚ÄúI left the studio so late, I had no time to go home in between. Perhaps you could find me something?‚Äù

Jenny conversed with Mrs.¬ÝGram about the price of embroidery patterns at home and in Paris, and about books she had lent her. Gram was reading. Now and again she felt his eyes on her. Helge returned about eleven.

‚ÄúWhat is the matter?‚Äù he asked, when they walked down the stairs. ‚ÄúHas there been a scene again?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, not at all,‚Äù she replied, in a short, irritated voice. ‚ÄúI suppose your mother did not like my coming home with your father.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt seems to me, too, that you need not have done it,‚Äù said Helge humbly.

‚ÄúI am going home by tram.‚Äù Overwrought, and unable to control herself, she pulled her arm out of his. ‚ÄúI cannot stand any more tonight, and I will not have these scenes with you every time I have been to your home. Good night.‚Äù

‚ÄúJenny! Wait! Jenny!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶‚Äù He hurried after her, but she was already at the stop when the tram came, and got in, leaving him without a word.
